author: @n1ch0las51
text:
This is a great channel. 
Thanks Mr Murray-Smith I Totally agree. 
I worked for a Marine Mammal Research Institute fir 10 years in the 80's I was the only "Technical" person on the team but some of the ideas the Bioligists came up with during our many brainstorming sessions were fantastic. We developed lots of specalist equipment, VHF direction finding using programmable single side band amateur radio recievers and a switched antenna array for example. Developing and using self powering remote recording stations left out on remote islands for months recording animal movement data. Wind power was often in the mix we relied on Lorry batteries to see us through the times when no sun or wind. The issue in the Faulklands was actually to much and to strong winds which burned out the generators. this was resolved on site in the middle of a remote island group by repairing the generators and then redesigning and jury rigging the stalling mechanism. Thinking out of the box was an essential requirement.

author: @williamhoward8732
text: With the Tesla turbine the disks are connected to the drive shaft. With your stacked cones they appear to be placed in a fixed position with the intent to force a greater volume of air to the rotor. Would it not be more efficient to incorporate the spinning cones with the rotor. I don't know off hand how the stack would be supported. I'm sure given some time I could solve that issue. Anyways just a thought. Love your videos Robert never miss a one.

author: @thatcheapguy525
text:
sounds like someone has got to you Robert. don't let them, you are quite a phenomenon. your channel is priceless intelligent and your delivery would be well placed in any worthy technical educational department.

growing up the son of a master mechanic I specialised early on and became a master engine builder in my youth. even after a season of top level race engine building I got bored. by pure luck the tool-hire industry came along and I became a jack of all trades (you had to be 40 years ago) and enjoyed decades at it. if I was to meet my young self today I would tell him "being a jack of all trades and master of none is often far more fulfilling than believing you are a master of one"

author: @neonsynth
text:
I've said it over and over since studying DaVinci in high school, "overspecialization is the death of creation."  By diversifying not only my skillsets but also my knowledge base I've created a networked mass of data in my head that can be drawn upon to solve the tricky situations.  It allows for more creative thinking, but most importantly it engenders the very idea of innovation even going to first principles if necessary. Thus was the Renaissance Man, known modernly as a polymath - born.
Innovation and invention in its truest form draws upon the best of the knowledge, data, and ideas already in existence and moves them along - ostensibly some further steps forward.  At times that requires starting a working theory from the ground up.  At other times it requires a broad top-down view of the entirety of the problem.  It is through broadening of our vision via knowledge accumulation that we can see more clearly, providing a framework of multiple field synthesis upon which we can build those limitless ideas of innovation.  Fantastic video Robert, thank you for sharing.

replies.author: @neonsynth
replies.text: Your work is singular in that it's one of the few channels that have provided me nearly as much joy through reading the comments and the inspiration you create in others as I get from the videos themselves.  That's the real beauty in your work.  You help others to see outside of a narrow, regimented line of thinking, and you do so with a lighthearted smile.  Keep up the great energy friend, brightens my mood every time.

author: @stss5755
text: Been watching your videos like crazy, mostly for the reasons you disclose in your jack of all trades speech. Thanks to you I've been jumping from electrolysis, to wind turbines; I've even made Solar cells from scraped phone screens. Here's a idea,one you can do while printing. can you use scraped lawn lanterns (the solar panels on top) to make a source of power to begin electrolysis. I've seen people use solar to pump water on the roof as a crude capacitor to then syphon it back down to run a hydro alternator of some sort... Anyways, my idea is to basically make a engine or generator that runs on hydrogen, which gets started with the solar power. Looking back on your older videos I remember you basically pulled a lot of you're materials from the trash bin so it would be interesting to see you utilize the panels on top of lawn lanterns and maybe other parts that come with it. I appreciate your efforts to teach the world about electronics, engineering, and especially the philosophy on how it's applied, keep up the fantastic videos

author: @mikedodger7898

author: @SilentDeath002
text: Hello.  enjoy watching your videos.  For over 15 years i have been making axial wind turbines that produce over 75 amps in 28mph wind and still produce a fair amount from 3 mph up to 12.  Question for you, have you tried to set up your coils in Star or Delta by matching your coils sets in 3 sets.  and basically making a  3 phase generator. you would have to center , phase 1 so all those coils hit the same orientation on each coils magnet , and so on for each phase of coils, hooking the phase up to bridge rectifier you should see a much higher volt and amp out put. i do have some youtube videos showing the output in a 28mph wind if you care to see it, also the build of the wind turbine. feel free to try it out, looking forward to your builds, i think your on to something with your designs especially for low wind which happens more then high winds do. have fun!

author: @Smo1k
text:
Being a Jack of All Trades is a talent expressing itself. Not the talent of long-term focus on a very specific subject (which leads to mastery), but the talent of having more room on the back burner than a master uses: Almost every person I've met that knows a little bit of everything probably remembers what they did with that spool that didn't work as a replacement for that motor, they were trying to repair 20 years ago, and that the reason it didn't work then is the reason it /may/ work in this magnetic levitation thing, they're tinkering with now...

You could say that it's the talent of finding most things interesting enough to remember 😉

(Example: Look to the medical industry for 02 generation as a way to avoid having N2 in the intake charge of a diesel engine to avoid NO pollutants).

author: @moistfinger
text: I love your enthusiasm , such an enjoyable watch and interesting too .

author: @Jaronius
text: Awesome!  I've had this view of progression in a discipline as Novice -> Expert -> Master -> Artist. The novice starts with nothing and learns from the master. The expert takes the lessons and perfects his craft then becomes the master. The master organizes his knowledge so the novice can more easily learn. The artist sits on the fringes, making new discoveries that change the craft forever. The artist is a novice beyond his craft.

author: @stevennope8864
text: Speaking of ideas, I was wondering about the magnetic bearing issues you were talking about a few videos ago. What if a thrust bearing was between the magnets? Then the magnets would provide load reduction, and thus reduced friction, but it wouldn't be fully supporting the weight. I would think that would reduce friction and increase longevity of the bearing, while limiting the downsides of a magnetic bearing, ie balancing. Haven't tinkered with it yet personally, so just tossing out an idea at this point. Might not be able to use a metal bearing, I don't know if any eddy currents would be generated and reduce the benefits. Also not sure but the practicality of using magnets strong enough to substantially reduce load with a great enough distance between them to fit a bearing. Maybe using a metal bearing would work better since it could provide a path for the magnetic fields to be close together. Great work as always Rob, looking forward to your future wind turbine designs.

author: @CrzyMan_Personal
text: This man is my ADHD goals. Such a great attitude on flitting about between projects, but actually managing them well. The world is built for and by people who do best starting and finishing one thing at a time. People who's brains a different like ours work better when we have a lot of things to work on. We allow our attention to flow. No walls. No brakes. We can go to some pretty amazing places if that energy is directed well.

In other words, you are NEVER VERY FAR from a WHOLE LOT of carbon black, should that fulfill your research needs!

Its thrown out by the MEGATONS!!!

ITS EVERYWHERE!!!

Had I the money to open up a waste recycling facility, I could EASILY be producing TONS [literally] of carbon black EACH AND EVERY SINGLE DAY!!!

And it would THEN NOT be put into LANDFILLS, OR into the OCEANS [where most of it ends up, eventually].

Just imagine, if people cooperatively agreed to collect all of the waste polystyrene cups out there and send it to some place where it could be easily converted into carbon black, for the purpose of advancing renewable energy research!

author: @ronniepirtlejr2606
text:
Robert you haven't answered my question from past videos.  
 I have asked you several times if you have any idea  what makes a  Fork made from a branch on a tree,  pull down to a water pipe or, just water? 
Some people call it witching for water. Why,  I have no idea?
The thing is Robert it really works!
I remember playing with these sticks that I made from a evergreen tree.
I would break the branch into a Y shape, pull apart & hold at knee level. 
When I would reach the water pipe buried under the ground, a very strong force would pull it down.  It was impossible for me to keep the branch level. 
What causes this effect?
You have any idea?
There is a tire Source there they can be tapped into!
They call it "witching for water" to make you believe it is not real.
 I believe science needs to study this!
P.S. if you don't know what makes it work then just say, ...
 I don't know mate!👍

text: I develop concepts and theories which are abstract in  thought like a machine. Each part of the theory is like a mechanical component. So when I take analytical data and formulas, I try to incorporate all the peculiarities of the subject, so I don't force a solution, based on expertise and ego and allow for modularity in my theory. Like Newton;s law F=Ma that;s fine formula until you start appro aching the speed of light then a whole set of paradigms emerge which we haven't experienced or discovered completely. So being humble,jack of all trades and always asking questions make the journey fun.

author: @curiositatibus2846
text:
Truly talented specialists are often surprisingly knowledgeable about many things.  

In my experience, it is less able specialists who are riddled with dogma often having a tenuous understanding of their subject and a sign of a lack of relative intellect.  

Its always worth listening to a specialist to make sure one isn't re-inventing the wheel.  One can always ignore their advice.

However, if ideas are not brought to some form of full execution, how can we know if they were any good?

author: @heathcliffebird7514
text:
One more thing.... coupling the method of energy production to the need rarely happens. my freezer works harder in summer and gets opened more during daylight hours. So it could mainly use solar energy during peak solar energy production with minimal need for battery capacity. 

Or wind power with a ton of really expensive batteries. 

You were bang on right about wind turbulence in urban areas being not what off the shelf wind generators work well with, so optimising for the conditions you have is a quick win. 

Matching the conditions that create energy with how and when theyre needed is a quick win. Reduces storage needs?

author: @SoundzAlive1
text: Hi Rob, The best example I remember is about the Physics student who was late to a lecture and copied what was written on the blackboard. A week or two later he handed in a solution to an equation he copied from the blackboard. That equation was shown to the class as an example of an unsolvable equation and students were told not to attempt to solve it. Without the constraints of thinking 'it can't be done' is what led to it being solved. Someone may know about this example and comment I hope. André in Sydney

text: Great explanation, and exactly how I feel too. Polymath is a good term, so is ignorance is bliss, but the majority of people are neither, just masters of one. Leaders, especially, need to be masters of all things. So glad you're open source, that in itself is a technology worthy of mastership!

author: @Gizmoautismo
text: I do my best to understand and be able to do well in as many branches of things as I can. Repairing and fixing random equipment has always been a really big part of my lifestyle, really. I obviously am not good enough to make everything from scratch and I don't have to be - it's all good to build on top of the shoulders of giants until you feel confident enough you can do something new safely and well enough for it to be viable. A few days ago I picked up a broken sewing machine for scrap value - what can I say, I have had no previous experience with sewing machines, but now it's not broken anymore because I figured out what was wrong with it, made a pair of brass gears from scrap and it's been really entertaining to do something well outside of my expertise for a while. I will obviously never be as good as my grandmother with them, but it doesn't matter - it's the fun of the process and the new possibilities that count. Funny enough my most developed area of engineering (in general power converters and UPSes) was discovered by me totally accidentally, some guy sent me a broken "crappy" UPS instead of a monitor and he said not to bother with sending it back. Now after a few years it's not just a "branch", but a whole tree where I can stick different experiences onto the fairly "simple" topic of UPSes - add homebrew batteries, custom power converters and other whacky solutions and the next thing I know I made something new! You have been a great inspiration to me for a good while, it really puts a smile on my face when you make these more general / life centered videos... not that the regular ones do not!

somewhere I have a badge that says JOAT on it that was presented to me, that said I have just set up another work bench, thats the fourth workbench because they soon get filled up with projects, currently I am working on a wind turbine / generator, a model boat (working on the anchor winch at the moment), I have ordered parts to try out connecting a spare solar panel to a heater element to help heat the hot tub, and the list goes on, something fires in my brain and i have to test it out, I have good cad skills (I should do, I have spent over 40 years in the trade) but I still dont consider myself a master, oh i know a lot, but i try and learn more every day and i try and help others learn too (thats important to me, passing along the skills).

author: @krissfemmpaws1029
text:
Here in the States much value is placed on the engineering staff, you know the over educated people that are trained to think inside a box. They are happy to tell people on the shop floor how to do their jobs while not knowing how to do the job itself. I have seen this acted out many times at different places of employment over the years.

Said engineers have a habit of making things more complex than they need to be, while the shop floor looks at how simple and easy can the job be done. 

When the shop floor suggests a better way to do something engineering diggs in their heels to fight the floor. 

An example of this one shop had a large press, to change between jobs took 24 hours. Management asked the engineers to come up with a better way to change and setup the dies. They also asked the shop floor to work on the problem. The engineers came back with their solution to trim about 4 hours off the set-up time and it would cost 150,000 dollars per tool set.

The shop floor came up with a system where they could change the dies in 3 to 4 hours and cost 1500 to 3000 dollars per tool set if that. 

The engineers were told if they wanted to keep their jobs they better get on the shop floor and get their hands dirty with the floor crew.

Two or three engineers quiet over that... one of the best resources engineering has is the people on the shop floor yet many refuse to use it.

An engineer friend of mine once said "Good engineers copy good ideas, bad engineers copy bad ideas." This is a good example of not being able to think outside the box!

author: @ogi22
text:
Personally i don't think your point of view is so rare (but this is my personal oppinion). My background has always been physics as far as i can remember. And i got my basic problem solving from my dad, who is a mechanical engineer. We often joke that he is the guy who deals with screws and i always preferred to chase electrons in wires ;) But such wide range of skillset is one of the best for a service tech. No matter what kind of machines you deal with. There is always a mechanical component, electronics and programming. Ability to see where is the problem, it is VERY precious thing. And there is no better service guy than jack of all trades - knowing how forces, tensions, temperatures etc. affect the process. Knowing what electronics components may be prone to failure (or simply how things work - eg. how inverter group is sharing energy and such). And knowing how things should be programmed to find flaws not related to mechanics and electronics. 
Actually a few years ago there was a field of engineering studies created at my former polytechnic, exactly to match those needs - mechatronics. Everything in one, all in moderation. Just to train those special "jacks of all trades" who can deal with problems in modern machines.

replies.author: @simontemplarGB
replies.text: @screen-protector Not having all the gear or knowledge that Robert has and being more interested in an appropriate practical solution I am more interested in the end than getting there since I have other interests that are for the journey.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: What is your most power-hungry electricity use? PV, VAWT, Solar/heat concentrator, Large battery storage, 2-inverters (one for lighting and small duty i.e. laptops etc, and one for 'power' i.e. cooking). Also heat scavenging system in your drains (throwing waste heat down the drain from showers/washing etc is a considerable and avoidable expense). Cross-flow heat exchangers in your ventilation. Insulate your home. Plus passive heat capture.

replies.author: @screen-protector
replies.text:
@simontemplarGB it meant to be sarcastic :D, hope you don't mind ;). 

Everything you need is online. You don't need to follow Robert ;). If you are here, that's the journey ;). I've built my first solar panel, the worst in the world, but charging my 18650's with no problem and several per day.

I could go and buy a system for a few thousands, why not? But, I've got no place to put it ;) and need to consider other production than only sun. I can put a max of about 1kW of solar panels and I'm thinking about the wind as a 2nd solutions.

I don't know your needs and I don't know what you're up to ;). I know my needs and I love to learn on the way ;). And, you can be you, the best you can ;).

replies.author: @screen-protector
replies.text:
@bikerfirefarter7280 in order to do it, you need to have funds ;). Not everyone has them, and not even everyone owns a house. 

So, although your answers are great, they do not apply to all.

The rest can do something, or do nothing. I like to do something to not be 100% depended on what's around me. 

I love the idea of saving 20-50% on my bills if possible ;) and that makes me happy. And, I think most of the people if would be able to cut their bills 20-50%, they'd be happy ;).

Now, when you've got 20-50% of savings, you can decide if you want to reinvest them, or just save up when you need to maintain your new system ;).

Or, learn to earn more and forget about all there is in those savings ;). You won't need to save up anymore. 

There's always a solution to the problem. But, here is a journey. Not the ready solution. That's how I see it. :)

replies.author: @ogi22

This approach is the base for "brainstorming". If you have a difficult problem, you first gather ALL ideas, no matter how stupid they might seem at first glance. And then you sort those ideas, attack or deffend them.
I think this is how they came up with that "stupid" idea of deicing powerlines with helicopters - flying very low over power lines to knock off ice buildup. Dangerous, but very effective:)

Keep it up Rob.  We are with ya

author: @revohirst
text: I was recommended this channel from you tube while looking for videos on how to reuse an old phone battery to power LEDs for some wargaming terrain.  I have been faithfully watching for about 2 years now.  I appreciate the content, but even more I appreciate your ethos.  Keep it up!

author: @dustintoth8632

author: @waynesmith2287
text: A mainframe computer vendor in the 1990's had problems with one computer for 3 days. It would randomly fail for no apparent reason. The data centre manager a Woman called her engineers in to her office. Told them she had enough and she was going to solve and fix the problem herself. She walked in to the computer room stood there for a few seconds walked out and said I can see what is wrong and this is how to fix it. Do it right now. Engineers said there is no way she was right but they did as they were told and it worked successfully. After that they had a meeting and she had a paper on the desk which read. "At quick glance I could tell the unit was not leveled properly and had moved slightly by vibration so likely loose cards in the frame." They asked how she came up with this in seconds. She said I was taught to think smart by a guy (me) who said The impossible just takes a little longer! I taught her well and we are still close friends to this day. My nick name is "Fountain of Knowledge"

author: @jamest.5001
text: I have worked in almost every trade, from concrete, carpentry, electrical, mechanical, and love to know how things work,  I could build a house, and a car if needed,

author: @camlegs2423
text: When I have a problem I like to ask kids or people who know nothing about what I'm trying to sort out! They see the obvious That I can miss at times lol

author: @davidprocter3578
text: The trouble with tunnel thought is that it inhibits lateral thought and blinkers the user from answers close to hand focusing the mind on the problem not the solution, It has a side effect commonly called cloth eared, a form of detrimental selective hearing, brought on by egocentric belief systems.I have come across a lot of this over the years, it leaves the sufferer with brain locks preventing the comprehension of the most obvious and basic explanations. Often bringing on inappropriate temper tantrums and initiating further brain lock. Fortunately this world is loaded with lateral thinkers, seek them out no matter how weird, the blinkered are only ever going to the same destination.

author: @K9JT
text:
Wow Rob, how far you've come from annealing copper with the back of a spoon in your wife's kitchen, uploading videos recorded on a potato 😛
It's been such an inspiring and exciting journey to watch. Well done on building a thriving community of tinkerers and for working so hard to share your knowledge and for encouraging others to do the same.

author: @philipvecchio3292
text:
I heard someone talking about this with sports. Some children are so hyper focused on one sport at a young age that it means that their muscles don't develop more generally.

But also like the comparison to the potato famine, monoculture caused more destruction than if they were planting multiple varieties of potatoes.

author: @waynesmith2287

author: @planet_Nige
text: Right Robert, I love your open access open minded attitude.  So here's a thought : a linear motor as suspension for a car. I would love to explore it but I have no time . However your followers might have the time. Think of it, every car with four shock absorbers generating energy as they drive. Now this has been expressed it can't be patented. Best wishes Nige.

author: @wodgesad
text: Love this! This is what Britain needs to make cool again the man in his shed. If it works it ain’t stupid whatever the experts say. I’ve made a very good living in software engineering with this viewpoint.

author: @timphillips3275
text: Thanks Mate, i needed that; i was down on myself for seaming to not finish a project, because i got so many things going; Yeah, '....it's  the journey.'

replies.author: @kevinlonghurst631
replies.text: I'm in plague with many of the same things you described over several videos. Know the feeling of getting down also. I have not been able to actualize much of anything lately with back problems from an extra vertebrae. I'm very much a Hands-On guy and get very little of it in the past 5yrs. Sure have been enjoying your videos! They cheer me up, the way and energy to which you concisely describe is awesome. Perhaps a feedback loop of cheering others up while being real and practical is a great thing! Keep at it! (Kevin from Idaho, usa)

author: @2010joen
text: I have found in my own experience that a problem in one "trade" may not be found in that "trade". The solution to that problem is often found in a "trade" you may never have heard of and is totally unrelated to the "trade" with your problem in it. So having even a casual acquaintance with many "trades" gives you a wider net you can cast to snag and catch the answer to your problem. Thank you for expanding our acquaintances.

author: @waynesmith2287
text: I was brought up to be a jack of all trades by a master in his field. My brother and sisters the same way. There is nothing we can't do between us. I became a master in 1 field cause I could. Retiring in a few months and I have enough projects to keep me going for a few decades.

author: @DurpVonFronz
text: A wide or broad intelligence is much better then singular or mastering one thing, as they will bottle neck or just fail outright when trying to figure out anything out of that mastery field. Ive know book worms who were very intelligent and could retain much, but get them to get on a snowboard or a bike, or even using a hammer and trying to hit a nail and its was hilariously painful to watch, totally stupid at it. like next level stupid. Super smrt but painfully stupid. lol

author: @colleenforrest7936
text:
So, I was just looking at a video by James Pray and he was 3D printing a regenerative air compressor.

He was using a motor to work the compressor but I was wondering if on a nice stormy day, the Darwin could spin the impeller in his device to compress air while it was also generating live electricity, and then the compressed air could be pumped back into your squirrel cage on less windy days as needed???

author: @mrdewilliams
text: I am so happy to hear someone else give the rest of the phrase.  I have always proudly declared "I'm a Jack of all trades, skillful at some.  I'll talk to a master when I start feeling dumb."

replies.text: Now that I think about this, it's so true.  Talking to a so called expert has gotten me no where.  I always go back, do te hard work, and figure out my answer to whatever problem I'm working on.

I have difficulty letting glue or paint dry between steps and often get involved with ANOTHER project and forget the one thats now dry and collecting dust
